clean_story:
Hidden labs. MIT’s unseen experiments.

The name MIT’s secret labs refers to classified or low-profile research spaces at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology—where projects too risky, weird, or early-stage for public scrutiny take shape. Like a scientist’s sketchbook, these labs test ideas before they’re ready for textbooks.

One example: The Rad Lab (1940–1945), hidden in Building 20, a "temporary" WWII wooden shack. Here, physicists rushed to invent radar jamming tech, using microwaves to blind enemy systems—a hack that later birthed the microwave oven. Another: The Media Lab’s "Counterculture Corner" in the 1980s, where researchers built wearable computers from bike helmets and sewing machines, decades before smartwatches.

These labs thrive on controlled chaos. No fixed budgets. No tenure pressure. Just whiteboards covered in equations, half-built robots, and the hum of 3D printers running at 3 AM. Failure isn’t punished; it’s logged. A 2017 leak revealed one team spent six months teaching AI to play Pong with a banana as a controller—seemingly silly, until the same code later improved prosthetic limb responsiveness.

Three related subjects are DARPA’s moonshot projects, Bell Labs’ golden era, and how universities hide breakthroughs.
